idea-plugin.name: spek - OpenSpec Viewer
idea-plugin.description:
spek is an OpenSpec content viewer that lets you browse specs, changes, and task progress directly within your IDE.

Features:

  • Specs Browser — Browse spec documents with BDD syntax highlighting (WHEN/GIVEN, THEN, AND, MUST/SHALL)
  • Changes Browser — View changes with a tab per artifact the change contains (custom OpenSpec schemas supported), with the change's schema shown as a badge; rows surface creation/archive dates and lifecycle duration
  • Timeline — Horizontal Gantt-style chart of every change's lifecycle, with optional spec-topic grouping and an auto-scaling time axis
  • Task Progress — Track task completion with section-grouped progress bars
  • Graph View — Interactive force-directed graph showing spec-change relationships
  • Full-text Search — Search across all specs and changes
  • Spec Diff — Compare current spec content against historical change versions
  • Live Reload — Automatically refreshes when openspec files change on disk
  • Dark / Light Theme — Syncs with your IDE theme

Supported IDEs: IntelliJ IDEA, WebStorm, PyCharm, PhpStorm, GoLand, RubyMine, CLion, Rider, and other IntelliJ-based IDEs (2023.3+)

Getting Started: Open a project that contains an openspec/ directory. The spek tool window will appear in the right sidebar.
idea-plugin.change-notes:
1.5.0

  • Custom OpenSpec schemas — a change's artifacts are now discovered from disk, so a change authored under any schema (not just the built-in spec-driven) renders every artifact as its own tab. Previously anything outside proposal / design / tasks / specs was silently dropped. Full-text search now indexes every markdown artifact as well.
  • Artifact tab ordering — tabs default to last-modified order so the artifact you're actively editing surfaces first. A sort control adds Schema order (sourced from the OpenSpec CLI, degrading gracefully to the default order when the CLI is unavailable or the change is archived) and A–Z. The choice persists across changes.
  • Schema badge — each change's schema is shown as a badge on the Changes list, the Dashboard, and Change Detail. It is hidden when the change uses the repo's default schema (from openspec/config.yaml), so a badge always means "this change uses a non-default schema"; the Changes page states the repo default as a plain-text label.
  • Thanks to Norman Hansen (@nthansen) for contributing all of the above.
